4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Software development has always evolved.
We moved from manual deployment to cloud platforms.
From monolithic applications to microservices.
From writing every line by hand to powerful frameworks that automate repetitive work.
Now AI is changing another part of software engineering.
Code can often be generated in seconds.
Documentation can be drafted instantly.
Tests can be suggested automatically.
This has led to one recurring question:
Will AI replace software engineers?
A more useful question is:
What skills become more valuable when writing code becomes easier?
Software Engineering Was Never Just About Typing Code
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Many people outside the industry imagine programming as writing syntax.
Experienced developers know that’s only a small part of the job.
A software project begins long before the first function is written.
It starts with understanding people.
What problem exists?
Who experiences it?
How should success be measured?
If those questions are answered incorrectly, perfectly written code still creates a poor product.
AI accelerates implementation.
It doesn’t automatically improve understanding.
A Practical Example
Imagine two teams building the same school management platform.
Both use modern AI coding assistants.
Team A
They immediately begin generating:
• authentication
• dashboards
• reports
• notifications
• analytics
• AI chatbot
Development progresses rapidly.
Three weeks later they demonstrate the product.
Teachers ask for a faster attendance workflow.
Parents ask for clearer fee tracking.
Administrators request simpler permissions.
Most of the generated functionality wasn’t solving their biggest daily frustrations.
Team B
Before writing code, they spend time understanding workflows.
They observe daily tasks.
They identify repetitive work.
Only then do they begin implementation.
They build fewer features initially.
But each feature solves a real problem.
The difference wasn’t better AI.
The difference was better product understanding.
AI Changes the Cost of Building
Historically, software teams avoided experimentation because building features was expensive.
Today the cost of producing code has decreased.
That changes an important business decision.
The expensive part is no longer generating code.
The expensive part is choosing the wrong problem.
Building unnecessary functionality is easier than ever.
Removing unnecessary functionality is still difficult.
The New Competitive Advantage
As AI improves, several skills become even more important.
Problem Definition
A clear problem statement saves weeks of development.
Instead of asking,
“Can we build this?”
teams should first ask,

“Should we build this?”
Communication
Developers increasingly work alongside designers, clients, testers, analysts, and AI tools.
Clear communication prevents misunderstandings that no model can automatically fix.
System Thinking
Applications rarely exist in isolation.
Every feature affects authentication, security, databases, APIs, infrastructure, performance, accessibility, and long-term maintenance.
AI can assist with components.
Engineers remain responsible for the whole system.
Validation
Generated code should never be accepted simply because it compiles.
Professional software teams still need:
• testing
• code review
• security validation
• performance evaluation
• accessibility review
Speed without verification creates technical debt.
AI Doesn’t Remove Responsibility
If an application exposes sensitive information, users won’t blame an AI model.
They’ll blame the product.
If a payment workflow fails, customers won’t ask which assistant generated the code.
They’ll judge the business delivering the software.
Responsibility remains human.
That makes engineering judgment more valuable, not less.
Lessons for Students
Students sometimes wonder whether learning fundamentals still matters.
It does.
Understanding algorithms, databases, networking, APIs, architecture, debugging, and software design makes AI dramatically more useful.
Without fundamentals it’s difficult to recognize when generated code is inefficient, insecure, or simply incorrect.
AI becomes a multiplier.
The foundation still matters.
Lessons for Founders
Founders should view AI as a way to accelerate validation rather than an excuse to build every possible feature.
Useful questions include:
• What is the smallest version customers can use?
• Which workflow wastes the most time today?
• What assumption should be tested first?
• Which manual process deserves automation?
Those questions create better products than larger feature lists.
Lessons for Development Teams
Successful engineering teams increasingly combine three capabilities.
They understand users.
They use AI to remove repetitive work.
They maintain strong engineering discipline.
That balance allows them to deliver quickly without sacrificing quality.
Rather than replacing collaboration, AI makes collaboration even more important because implementation becomes faster than decision-making.
A Practical Decision Framework
Before starting the next feature, ask five questions.
1. What specific problem are we solving?
2. Who benefits most?
3. How will success be measured?
4. What is the smallest useful version?
5. How will we validate the result?
If these questions are answered clearly, AI becomes an accelerator instead of a distraction.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Final Thoughts
Every major technology shift changes how software is built.
Few eliminate the need for thoughtful engineering.
AI is no exception.
The organizations that benefit most won’t necessarily be those generating the largest amount of code.
They’ll be the ones consistently solving meaningful problems, validating assumptions, maintaining quality, and building software people genuinely want to use.
Technology changes rapidly.
Sound engineering principles remain remarkably consistent.
For companies such as TechMigos, that means combining modern development tools with disciplined workflows, practical problem-solving, and close collaboration with stakeholders so technology serves real business needs rather than becoming an end in itself.
